Title: Kingdom of the Wicked (Kingdom of the Wicked, #1)
Author: Kerri Maniscalco
My Review:
This book, guys, I don't know how she did it, but it must have crack in it somewhere. It had me from the very first page to the very last. I was so sucked in I would've read it in one go if I had had the time. The atmosphere was absolutely on point, with the witchy vibes arriving just in time for Autumn.

I really liked the way the seven sins are represented as the princes and their households. While we didn't get an extensive backstory or information about them in this book, it left me curious for more in-depth exploration in the next book.

I loved the chemistry between Emilia and Wrath. What's not to like about a brooding though handsome MC who's evil towards everyone but has a soft spot for this one girl? It's a very slow burn, though, so you have to be patient. It reminded me a lot of the Folk of the Air series by Holly Black. If Jude and Cardan did it for you, you're gonna love this one.

But here's the one thing I HATED - the ending! Now I have to pick up the next book ASAP, which is annoying because I have so many other books on my TBR waiting to be read, and I'm also on a shopping ban

Title: Anatomy: A Love Story (The Anatomy Duology, #1)
Author: Dana Schwartz
My Review:
I'm so in love with this book! The dark academia vibes, the historical setting, the empowering female main character! I was hooked, even through my lack of time to read. And since I visited Edinburgh recently, reading about familiar places made it even better. I felt like I was back there as I went through the story.

Hazel Sinnett, our fearless protagonist, dreams of being a surgeon more than getting married. After getting barred from medical lectures for her gender, she strikes a deal with renowned surgeon Dr. Beecham: pass a medical exam to continue her career. Without formal lessons, Hazel needs corpses, and Jack, a grave-digger, fits the bill. However, they face more than anatomical challenges—mysterious disappearances and a resurgent Roman Fever.

The love story was just right – not too over the top, which made it all the more powerful. It's a forbidden love/Rich vs poor/ unequal social status trope book. Which I wouldn't normally go for but I enjoyed it so much!

The last few chapters were tensive, I was glued to the pages. And that ending? It had me yelling! Now I'm itching to grab the second novel ASAP.

This book is a must-read, blending history, empowerment, and a story that grabs you and won't let go.

Title: Gobbolino, the Witch's Cat
Author: Ursula Moray Williams
My Review:
I picked up “Gobbolino the Witch’s Cat” because the cover was so cute, and the title had me at “witch’s cat.” I mean, anything involving cats and witchy vibes gets me immediately, especially with Halloween being do close. This book is a short and sweet read, perfect for a quick read between heavier books.

Considering it’s geared toward kids, I chose not to judge the book too harshly and instead enjoy Gobbolino’s adventure for what it was. Yes, it might be a bit repetitive at times, and the moral of the story might not be groundbreaking, but that wasn’t the point for me. What mattered was the heartwarming tale of a cat seeking his forever home. Sometimes, that’s all you need from a book.

Gobbolino stole my heart. If I could, I’d adopt him in a heartbeat!

Title: Murder at the Bookstore (The Bookstore Mystery, #1)
Author: Sue Minix
My Review:
I stumbled upon "Murder at the Bookstore" during one of my usual "I should really stop buying more books" strolls in the local bookstore. It wasn't hard to notice with its cute cover – a bookstore, a murder, and a cat? Sold!

Our main character is a budding author who's supposed to be working on her second book after a debut that hit big. But what's she doing instead? Battling the mother of all writer's blocks while getting involved in the investigation of the sudden death of her friend, the bookstore owner she saw every day. Throughout the story, she references the main characters of her book and wonders what they would do in the situations she's currently in, which is a nice touch.

The 'bad' relationship with her mom was so random, I kept thinking, "Is this relevant? Will it tie into the murder?" Nah, it doesn't. But hey, in the end, they miraculously go from endless bickering to one big happy family. All's well that ends well, I guess.

The story did pick up in the last 100 pages as we got closer to finding out whodunit. And what do you know, it's the exact person I guessed it would be in the first few pages. I kept hoping for a big plot twist I didn't see coming, but alas!

A heads-up: this book gets pretty graphic towards the end, which was unexpected given its cozy, autumn-mystery vibes. So, be warned if you're not into the gory stuff!

Conclusion: It was predictable but overall an entertaining read and a cute book.

Title: Kingdom of the Cursed (Kingdom of the Wicked, #2)
Author: Kerri Maniscalco
My Review: Omg.. This book, let me tell you, when I thought the first book of this series had me in a chokehold, I was wrong. Kingdom of the Cursed is the second book in the series and it gripped me into it's world even harder than the first. I mean, I could practically hear the hooves of Emilia's horse pounding the ground in certain scenes, like i was there. This series has seriously grown up – it's gone from YA to NA, and you can feel it from as early as chapter 2
